evals.prompt: You are reviewing C flight software for a student rocket's avionics board. Check this code snippet for compliance with NASA Power of 10 subset rules (no goto, no unbounded recursion, loops with static bounds), MISRA C essentials (no implicit signedness changes, initialize variables), and BARR-C guidelines (no static globals, use enums for state machines, return status from functions). The code implements an altitude sensor reading function:
evals.expected_output: The review should identify violations and provide fixes: 1) Replace any goto statements with structured error handling using return codes, 2) Ensure all loops have compile-time upper bounds, 3) Eliminate unbounded recursion by adding depth limits or converting to iteration, 4) Fix implicit signedness conversions with explicit casts, 5) Initialize all variables at declaration, 6) Remove static global variables or add static specifier, 7) Use enum types for state machines, 8) Ensure every function returns a status code indicating success/failure.
expectations: Identifies and flags goto statement usage
Checks for loops without static upper bounds
Detects unbounded recursion without depth limits
Flags implicit conversions that change signedness
Notes uninitialized variable usage
Identifies global variables lacking static specifier
Recommends enum types for state machine implementation
Verifies functions return status codes

evals.prompt: Design a flight state machine for a dual-deploy model rocket with the following flight phases: pre-flight (ground operations, sensor calibration), boost (motor ignition to burnout), coast (unpowered ascent to apogee), apogee (peak altitude), descent (drogue chute descent), recovery (main chute descent to ground), and landed (post-flight). The rocket uses redundant apogee detection (primary barometric, secondary accelerometer-based).
evals.expected_output: A state machine design with: 1) Enum defining all seven states (PRE_FLIGHT, BOOST, COAST, APOGEE, DESCENT, RECOVERY, LANDED), 2) State transition table showing guarded transitions (e.g., BOOST→COAST only on burnout detection, APOGEE→DESCENT only on apogee confirmation), 3) Entry/exit actions for each state (e.g., entering BOOST activates engine monitoring, exiting BOOST logs boost phase data), 4) Redundant apogee logic requiring both sensors to agree before transitioning to apogee state, 5) Error handling for sensor disagreements or premature transition attempts.
expectations: Defines enum with all seven flight states
Shows state transition table with guarded conditions
Specifies entry actions for each state
Specifies exit actions for each state
Implements redundant apogee detection logic
Includes error handling for sensor conflicts
Handles premature transition attempts safely
Uses descriptive state names matching flight phases

evals.prompt: Define a LoRa telemetry packet structure for a rocket flight computer that needs to transmit data from 4 sensors: pressure (4 bytes), temperature (4 bytes), acceleration (3 axes × 4 bytes each = 12 bytes), and GPS (latitude 4 bytes, longitude 4 bytes, altitude 4 bytes = 12 bytes). Total payload is 32 bytes. The system operates in the 915 MHz ISM band and must include error detection.
evals.expected_output: A LoRa packet specification with: 1) Preamble: 8+ bytes of 0x55 for synchronization, 2) Sync Word: 2 bytes (0x34 0x12 for LoRaWAN compatibility), 3) Header: 4 bytes explicit header (Address, Length, Packet Type, Flags), 4) Payload: 32 bytes containing sensor data in defined order, 5) CRC: 2 bytes CCITT-CRC for error detection, 6) Recommended configuration: SF7-SF12, 125 kHz bandwidth, 4/5 coding rate, 7) Frequency planning within 902-928 MHz band with specific channel recommendations.
expectations: Specifies 8+ byte preamble (0x55) for synchronization
Defines 2-byte sync word (0x34 0x12)
Describes 4-byte explicit header structure
Details 32-byte payload format for 4 sensors
Includes 2-byte CCITT-CRC for error detection
Recommends SF7-SF12 spreading factor range
Specifies 125 kHz bandwidth
Recommends 4/5 coding rate
Places frequency in 902-928 MHz ISM band
Provides channel spacing recommendation

evals.prompt: Create a unit test plan for testing a barometric altitude sensor driver function that converts raw pressure readings to altitude in meters. The function signature is: float calculate_altitude(uint32_t pressure_pa, float temperature_c). It uses the barometric formula with sea level pressure compensation. Test should cover normal operation, edge cases, and error conditions.
evals.expected_output: A test plan using the Unity framework with: 1) Arrange-Act-Assert structure for each test case, 2) Normal range test (sea level to 5000m altitude), 3) Edge cases: minimum pressure, maximum pressure, extreme temperatures, 4) Invalid input handling (out-of-range pressure values), 5) Mocked sensor interface tests, 6) Descriptive test names indicating what each test validates, 7) Test for temperature compensation accuracy, 8) Boundary value analysis for pressure inputs.
expectations: Uses Unity Arrange-Act-Assert pattern
Tests normal operating range (sea level to 5000m)
Covers edge cases (min/max pressure, temperature extremes)
Tests invalid input handling and error conditions
Includes mocked sensor interface tests
Features descriptive test names
Validates temperature compensation logic
Applies boundary value analysis to inputs
Tests sea level pressure compensation
Verifies function returns float altitude in meters
